summary: Split the early lbcommands/subaction-event bytecode handlers out of the generic early asm blob, introduced src/melee/lib/lbcommands.c and lbcommands.h, moved remaining handlers into asm/lbcommands_asm.s, exposed the lbcommands dispatch table in asm/data.s, and updated build paths/object ordering. The PR also documented that the lbcommands name is inferred from lb*.c asserts and that SubactionEvent names follow community convention plus command-byte hex.
what_changed: Added src/melee/lib/lbcommands.c containing SubactionEvent_00 in C and SubactionEvent_04 with a NON_MATCHING C version plus a matching inline-asm fallback.
Added src/melee/lib/lbcommands.h with the SubactionInfo struct and declarations for SubactionEvent_00 and SubactionEvent_04.
Split functions from the previous early asm blob into a dedicated asm/lbcommands_asm.s covering 0x8000594C through 0x80005BAC for the still-asm lbcommands handlers.
Renamed/truncated the remaining asm blob to asm/code_80005BB0.s so it starts at 0x80005BB0 instead of carrying the lbcommands range.

Replaced an incbin for lbl_803B9840 in asm/data.s with an explicit function-pointer table referencing SubactionEvent_00, SubactionEvent_04, func_80005994, func_800059DC, func_80005A30, func_80005A88, func_80005AC4, func_80005AE4, func_80005B00, func_80005B18, followed by zero entries.
Updated obj_files.mk to build lbcommands.o before lbcommands_asm.o and the remaining code_80005BB0.o.
Updated the Makefile SRC_DIRS and INCLUDES to add src/melee and src/melee/lib.
smart_moves: Kept object-file ordering explicit in obj_files.mk, which is critical when splitting a contiguous text range without changing addresses.
Separated known/partially understood lbcommands code from unrelated early asm instead of leaving it inside a broad address-named file.
Exposed lbl_803B9840 as a real function-pointer table, making the command dispatch relationship searchable and useful for later decompilation.
Used a NON_MATCHING C implementation for SubactionEvent_04 while preserving the matching inline assembly, allowing documentation of intent without sacrificing build match.
Grounded naming in available evidence: the PR body explicitly says the lbcommands source name is assumed from lb*.c asserts and the SubactionEvent names follow an old community convention.
decomp_lessons: When an address blob contains a coherent subsystem, split it into a named source/asm pair even if only a subset can be converted to C.
Build order is part of the match: splitting asm into C plus asm requires updating obj_files.mk in the same address order as the original code.
Converting incbin data into symbolic tables can clarify control flow and provide anchors for future matching, especially dispatch tables.
If a decompiled function is semantically clear but not matching, keep the C under NON_MATCHING and preserve exact inline assembly for the matching build.
Document uncertainty in file/module names; inferred names from asserts are useful but should be labeled as assumptions.
A shared struct used by later code can justify extracting an early generic library subsystem, but the PR cautions not to conflate this with Sysdolphin bytecode.c.
naming_conventions: The module name lbcommands is described as mostly assumed because nearby/asserted source names appear to be lb*.c files.
Function names use SubactionEvent_XX, where XX is the command byte in hex, based on an old community standard mentioned in the PR body.
The extracted struct is named SubactionInfo and fields are named by observed roles such as timer, frame_count, data_position, loop_count, EventReturn, loop_count_dup, and unk_x18.
Unidentified remaining functions kept address-based names such as func_80005994 and func_80005B64 rather than speculative semantic names.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Preserved exact assembly for non-decompiled handlers in asm/lbcommands_asm.s while moving matching/decompilable code into src/melee/lib/lbcommands.c.
Used inline asm for SubactionEvent_04 in the matching path because the available C version is marked NON_MATCHING.
Maintained the text layout by ordering lbcommands.o, lbcommands_asm.o, and code_80005BB0.o in obj_files.mk.
Replaced raw incbin data for lbl_803B9840 with symbolic .4byte entries to the handler functions, improving relocation/symbol visibility.
Adjusted the remaining asm section comment for code_80005BB0.s to start at 0x80005BB0 after removing the 0x80005940-0x80005BAC command-handler range.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR slice.
The only human rationale available is the PR body, which explains the assumed lbcommands name, SubactionEvent naming convention, shared struct observation, and distinction from Sysdolphin bytecode.c.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that decompile the remaining asm/lbcommands_asm.s functions.
Search for 8007121C and later users of the same SubactionInfo struct mentioned in the PR body.
Check whether SubactionInfo field names were later refined or renamed.
Search for additional SubactionEvent_XX handlers and whether the XX command-byte naming convention stayed consistent.
Compare this lbcommands bytecode reader against Sysdolphin bytecode.c only to avoid false equivalence noted by the author.
